title: Is excess linoleic acid contributing to mitochondrial dysfunction?

chunks.title: Fructose and Uric Acid Discussion
chunks.summary: The discussion begins with an exploration of fructose and its connection to uric acid. It delves into the enzyme aldose reductase, which converts glucose to fructose, and how this process can be problematic for people with diabetes and metabolic dysfunction.

chunks.title: Impacts of Seed Oils on Health
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the effects of seed oils, particularly linoleic acid, on mitochondrial dysfunction and overall health. As they accumulate in cell membranes, they can lead to various physiological problems, linking this to potential increases in metabolic dysfunction and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.
chunks.transcript: for linoleic acid but not in a good way meaning the more seed oils you put on your skin the more seed oils you put in your mouth the more seed oils you're going to find in your membranes and by seed oils i mean things like linoleic acid linoleic acid and 18 carbon omega-6 fatty acid that is a big problem because you are accumulating those we can look at the adipose tissue of humans and tell how much linoleic acid they've been eating because the adipose tissue stores linoleic acid it's stores it's a history book you can get rid of it when you decrease your linoleic acid gradually but if you are eating consistent amounts of linoleic acid i can look at your fat i can look at the cell membranes of your body and tell you how much linoleic acid you've been eating and that's a bad thing you are accumulating linoleic acid in corn canola safflower sunflower grapeseed soybean oils major problem you can also accumulate linoleic acid from chicken or pork that is fed corn and soy if their fat is enriched in linoleic acid which is why i'm a fan of eating a low linoleic acid diet that's why i'm such a fan of red meat red meat fat tallow etc high in steric acid high in odor chain fatty acids and low in linoleic acid i've done many podcasts on this you can recently uh you can refer to the recent one i did with mcbear who interviewed me on seed oils it's on my same podcast here fundamental health if you want more on the serial conversation but i do have some concerns and i think there's a real interesting possibility here that as we age

chunks.title: Diet Recommendations for Diabetics
chunks.summary: The speaker provides guidance for those with diabetes regarding dietary choices. They recommend a lower carbohydrate intake, cautioning against processed sugars and seed oils, while suggesting that moderate fruit consumption can be acceptable, particularly honey, which may improve insulin sensitivity.
chunks.transcript: diabetic and i think the answer is yes i do i don't think a lot of carbohydrates are going to be great for you i would probably keep your fruit intake to less than mine i don't think i would eat 150 to 200 grams of carbohydrates like i do from fruit and honey a day if you're diabetic but i think it's okay to get some in the past i've actually shown studies where they gave honey to diabetics and their insulin sensitivity improved their blood sugars went up but their insulin sensitivity got better with honey so i don't think honey is pushing you in the wrong direction getting blood sugar spikes might not be a great thing based on what i discussed earlier because your glucose might spike you might have a lot of aldosterones so i think in general trending toward lower carbohydrates is better if you're already sick if you're already diabetic i would decrease those things if you're metabolically healthy you can heat fructose you can eat fruit you can eat honey i would never eat sugar i would never eat processed sugar i never eat soda i would always know that those are the major problem they're often added to things and i would never eat seed oils if you're diabetic if you're pre-diabetic if you're obese do lower carbohydrate do 75 100 grams of carbohydrates a day see how you do check your a1c checks your fasting insulin check your fasting glucose wear continuous glucose monitor do all of those things and monitor but i think the lower carbohydrate diets could be better

chunks.title: Healing Through Diet and Lifestyle
chunks.summary: An emphasis is placed on the body's ability to heal itself through evolutionary-consistent diet and lifestyle choices. The speaker stresses the importance of removing harmful foods and toxins from the diet while advocating for nutrient-dense options such as organs, meat, honey, and raw dairy to promote optimal health.
chunks.transcript: they are fixable they are reversible when we return to an evolutionarily consistent state this is more of a naturopathic perspective i almost went to naturopathic medical school as most of you know i went to allopathic medical school i have an md from the university of arizona and i did residency at the university of washington so i did allopathic mainstream medical school but my thinking is more in line with natural apathy in the sense that i think that if we remove the impediments the body will heal itself into that i would add if you remove the impediments in this case perhaps processed sugar seed oils toxin containing vegetables which is a very controversial opinion that i hold and you give your body the nutrients it needs with organs meat fruit honey and raw dairy in that case your body will kill itself so do not believe do not become despondent do not believe that you cannot heal yourself with diet and lifestyle if you live evolutionarily that's what optimal health is all about that's what i'm about that's what i want to communicate to all of you that with movement with nature with proper sunlight and with nourishment properly you can heal and reverse your illnesses that's why i do this podcast that's why i'm excited to
